text:
This paper focuses on the phenomenon of bilingualism in the city of Cieszyn and Český Těšín, its Czech sister city, separated by the Olza River since 1920, after Cieszyn Silesia was divided into two states. The town and its surroundings have long-standing multi-ethnic tradition, yet the current linguistic situation on the Polish and Czech sides of the border is a result of different legislative and social circumstances. The paper analyses the communication of Polish and Czech in urban space, concentrating on the forms and purposes of labelling in street-furniture and on advertising boards.

Based on authentic language material, such as conversations recorded in marketplaces and texts on display boards, the study determines the most frequently occurring errors in communication between speakers of the two Slavic languages. The article is focused on spelling and punctuation mistakes, as well as on the morphological adaptation of words borrowed from other languages, expressions of politeness and diminutives, and interlinguistic homonyms. It also shows that language use is determined by the cultural context of communication and that while Czech bilingualism on the part of ethnic minorities is a formal and political expression of minority language rights, on the part of Poles it is an everyday pragmatic means of making business transactions possible across the border. The article finally demonstrates that the mental and material borderland is built into the very communication between the Czech and Polish Slavs.
